bibliographic_data.title: The Geometry of Coalition Power: Majorization, Lattices, and Displacement in Multiwinner Elections
bibliographic_data.abstract: How much influence can a coordinated coalition exert in a multiwinner Top-𝑘election under a positional scoring rule? We study this question through the lens of the maximum displacement problem: given a coalition of size 𝑚, how many of the current top-𝑘winners can be forcibly replaced? Our first contribution is a structural decomposition showing that coalition power reduces to two independent prefix-majorization constraints—one controlling how strongly the strongest outsiders can be boosted, and one governing how weak winners can be suppressed. For general positional scoring rules, these prefix inequalities give tight, efficiently checkable necessary conditions—i.e., they exactly capture the coalition’s score-supply limits in the continuous relaxation. We obtain an exact discrete characterization when the relevant score ladders are arithmetic-progression (AP) ladders with a common step size 𝑔 ('common-step AP ladders'), a broad class including Borda, truncated Borda, 𝑘-approval/𝑘-veto variants, plurality, and other multi-level positional rules. In this setting we prove an exact Majorization–Lattice Theorem: the coalition’s aggregate feasible score vectors are precisely the integer points that satisfy (i) the Block–HLP prefix-sum capacity constraints and (ii) a single global congruence condition modulo 𝑔. For pure Borda (𝑔= 1), the congruence constraint disappears and feasibility reduces to a pure prefix-majorization test.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper provides a structural decomposition of coalition influence in multiwinner elections, proving an exact Majorization–Lattice Theorem for common-step AP ladders and developing an efficient feasibility oracle.
core_content.key_methodology: Structural decomposition into prefix-majorization constraints and geometric characterization using the Block–HLP envelope.
outcomes: Exact discrete characterization of coalition power under common-step AP ladders
Development of a fast exact feasibility oracle for computing maximum achievable displacement
core_content.full_text_markdown: In multiwinner elections, coalitional manipulation—the ability of a group of voters to coordinate their ballots to alter an election outcome—is a central challenge. This paper studies the maximum displacement problem: given a coalition of size 𝑚, how many of the current top-𝑘winners can be forcibly replaced? The authors provide a structural decomposition showing that coalition power reduces to two independent prefix-majorization constraints—one controlling how strongly the strongest outsiders can be boosted, and one governing how weak winners can be suppressed. For general positional scoring rules, these prefix inequalities give tight, efficiently checkable necessary conditions—i.e., they exactly capture the coalition’s score-supply limits in the continuous relaxation. The paper also proves an exact Majorization–Lattice Theorem for common-step AP ladders, a broad class including Borda, truncated Borda, 𝑘-approval/𝑘-veto variants, plurality, and other multi-level positional rules. This theorem provides an efficient feasibility oracle to compute the maximum achievable displacement.
